observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to give utmost attention to the preparation and timely submission of the required monthly BRS, in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996, and to substantiate the unreconciled difference amounting to ₱24,861,246.35;
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and maintain updated Cash Books and ensure these are regularly reconciled with the accounting records; and
c. Consider employing additional personnel in the Accounting Office to support the timely preparation of BRS and the proper maintenance of SLs.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough tracing and analysis of the transactions recorded under the intra-agency accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds, reconcile the identified discrepancies, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to ensure the accuracy and fair presentation of the FS.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Prepare a certified schedule of dormant payable accounts that are aged over two years and for which there are no valid claimants or supporting documents. After proper validation, initiate the reversion of the verified amounts to the Unappropriated Surplus under the Government Equity account by preparing the necessary JEVs to effect the adjustments in the Municipality’s books of accounts, in accordance with COA Circular No. 76-45; and
b. Implement a tracking system for paybales or maintain an updated aging schedule to regularly monitor the status of all outstanding obligations to ensure that only valid, existing, and properly documented payables are recorded and retained in the Municipality’s books of accounts.

We recommended that Management:
a. Submit the lacking supporting documents, along with those DVs and ObRs that were not approved by the proper officials for the aforementioned financial transactions;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen its internal control in the pre-audit of financial transactions and ensure that supporting documents were complete, accurate, and duly approved by proper officials, in compliance with Section 4 (5) and (6) PD No. 1445; and
c. Consider creating a checklist of documentary requirements for advance, progress, and final billings to ensure that all supporting documents are complete before processing payments, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and other procurement related regulations; and
d. Submit explanations on the noted deficiencies.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the missing DVs and RDs, along with complete supporting documents; and
b. Hire additional competent staffs for the Accounting Office to expedite the processing and recording of voluminous financial transactions of the Municipality and ensure timely submission of all accounts within the prescribed period.

We recommended that Management:
a) Require the Inventory Committee to continuously make utmost effort to conduct a complete and detailed physical inventory of all the Municipality’s properties in accordance with the new guidelines and procedures prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006, prepare the RPCPPE and submit within the deadline; b) Require the Municipal Accounting Office concerned personnel to maintain complete and updated PPELCs to support General Ledger (GL) account balances and the General Service Office (GSO) concerned personnel to keep corresponding PCs for control purposes; c) Direct the Inventory Committee to immediately request authority to dispose of those identified unserviceable and obsolete PPE and drop from the books the book value thereof upon approval in accordance with the existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations; and d) Require the GSO and the Municipal Accounting Office to make a final reconciliation of their respective records and avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006, particularly on the disposition and derecogntion of non-existing/missing PPE and adjust the books accordingly, in order to come up with a reliable PPE balances.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant, the GSO and the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of the LRNLC, LRNPC and RPCLRN, respectively; 
Direct the Inventory Committee and GSO to prepare and submit the RPCLRN and RLRN, respectively, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose the total road network in the Notes to the FS in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that Management:
Submit to the Audit Team copies of the MOA and supporting documents on the subject FTs and ensure their submission for any subsequent fund transfers; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
i) Review, correct and update the identified schedules with negative balance and conduct periodical check for negative abnormal balances in the accounts and adjust accordingly;
ii) Evaluate the non-moving sub-account balances and facilitate the necessary liquidation/refund of unexpended balance of those projects to the Source Agency (SA);
iii) Submit to the concerned SA the liquidation reports and return/remit the outstanding balance, for completed projects, if warranted. If the disposition of the unspent/unutilized amount is not provided in the MOA, Trust Agreement or Memorandum of Understanding, the agency may, at their option, utilize the said amount by sending a letter to the SA requesting approval to transfer the unexpended balance to the GF. (Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001); and
iv) Reconcile the Due to NGAs account and take up the necessary correcting/adjusting entries for fair presentation in the FS.

We recommended the Management:
Direct all the concerned officials and employees to settle their CAs within the prescribed period;
File request for authority to write-off dormant or long outstanding CAs, if necessary, to the Office of the Auditor, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Refrain from granting additional CA unless the previous CAs are liquidated;
Issue demand letters to officials and employees, who have outstanding CAs as of December 31, 2023, especially those who are still in the active service with material amount including those individuals who are assigned in other LGUs or other offices. A copy of which must be submitted to the Audit Team with proof of service and dated to enforce settlement of unliquidated CAs; and
Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the CAs especially those accounts with abnormal and unreconciled balance and prepare an adjustment, if necessary.

We recommended the Management to require the AOs to have all collections as of year-end be remitted and deposited intact and in full amount with the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB). Henceforth, strict adherence to Section 69 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 31 and 32 of Manual on NGAS for LGUs is required.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace and analyze the pertinent transactions of the Intra-Agency accounts Due from/Due to Other Funds, reconcile the difference/s and prepare adjusting entry/ies, as necessary.

We recommended that the Agency Head instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the lacking/unsubmitted CY 2023 TBs, Financial Reports and Statements, DVs with complete supporting documents, collections accounts and other monthly reports as well as the lacking DVs from prior years. Otherwise, the subject disbursements will be disallowed in audit.
Henceforth, require the Municipal Officials concerned to comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of paid DVs, ORs, JEVs, FS and other Financial Reports.

We recommended that Management:
Require the District Supervisor to prepare the SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 pursuant to Section 5 of JC No. 1 s. 2017 and Section 100 (b) of RA No. 7160;
Ensure participation of the majority of the members of LSB in the formulation of SEF budget yearly and the formulation of three- year program of SEF budget taking into consideration the factors and other criteria indicated in the planning and budgeting for the SEF and incorporating the SIP and DEDP in collaboration with stakeholders in the community; and
Strictly comply with the guidelines on the planning and budgeting of SEF in accordance with Section 5 of JC No. 1 s. 2017 and Section 100 (b) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly adhere to the above cited provisions on the procurement of infrastructure projects. Henceforth, all perfected contracts together with its supporting documents must be submitted to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from execution of contract for the required auditorial review and technical evaluation; and
Direct concerned officials to submit lacking documents and the BAC Secretariat to maintain a checklist of required documents to be submitted and ensure that contracts for infrastructure projects is properly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management:
Inform the Audit Team within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted for the validation of the Audit Team; and
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Audit Team, at the beginning of the year, with a list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year including the required data/information as well as the monthly monitoring report on the Project Status (in Annex “A”) in accordance with Sections 2, 3.1 and 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and maintain RAAOs per function/program/project, allotment class, current/continuing appropriations and fund for proper monitoring of balances of available appropriations and allotments to avoid the incurrence of obligations in excess of available allotments/appropriations.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to facilitate the gathering of data and information needed for the creation of an updated and well-established GAD database to have an accurate and reliable basis in the planning and programming of PPAs as well as in the policy formulation in order to properly address the existing and emerging GAD issues and concerns;
Direct the GFPS and GAD key personnel to undergo capacity building programs and training on gender and development to enhance their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools like HGDG on the attributed budget to evaluate gender responsiveness of PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget; and
Require the GFPS to update the GAD Code in accordance with the guidelines prescribed by PCW.

We recommended that Management require:
The Municipal Accountant to:
adopt the Modified Accrual method of accounting for income from RPT and SET;
observe due diligence in the recording of transactions by using the appropriate accounts under COA Circular No. 2015-009 and following the series of proforma entries in taking up all transactions on RPT and SET; and
reconcile the affected accounts and effect the necessary adjusting/correcting entries to present fairly the balances in the Financial Statements;
The Municipal Treasurer to prepare and update the RPTAR/RPT Index Card and the Certified List of Taxpayers and submit the latter to the Accounting Office at the end of the year to be used as basis in establishing the RPT/SET Receivable; and
The Municipal Assessor to prepare and update the Assessment Roll for newly assessed or reassessed properties and furnish the Municipal Treasurer except only where the Assessor’s records are computerized and readily available to the Municipal Treasurer, thru a local area network.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Inventory Committee to completely fill out the information on the whereabouts and conditions of all properties physically counted to determine if these PPE items are already unserviceable and beyond repair or can be put to use if properly repaired or rehabilitated. The Inventory Committee must recommend for disposal those PPE items which are unserviceable and beyond repair while the properties still carry their respective salvage value and submit a written report to the GSO of the result of the actual physical inventory conducted so the latter could undertake appropriate and feasible remedies for any deficiencies noted; and
Dispose the identified unserviceable properties in accordance with COA Circular Nos. 89-296 and 92-386 and accordingly drop from the books the book value thereof in accordance with existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

We reiterated and recommended the following to Management:
Adherence to Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 on the withholding and remittance, intact, in full amount, and within the prescribed period of taxes due to the national government; and
Require the concerned officials to reconcile its records and with the records of the BIR and immediately remit the correct payables to enable the government to allocate resources for priority development programs.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team copies of the Memorandum of Agreement and supporting documents on the subject fund transfers and ensure submission of any subsequent fund transfers.

We recommended that Management stop the granting of reimbursement for fuel expenses regardless of whether or not a public official is provided with a service vehicle and to cause the refund of the amount claimed for reimbursement of fuel.
We also recommended that payment for fuel must be supported with complete and properly filled-up supporting documents specially approved Trip Ticket. Report of Fuel Consumption is also required to be submitted monthly to the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management:
Require recipient schools to submit approved SIP and DEDP of DepEd and other required supporting documents as a requisite for approval of funding support and to issue official receipt upon receipt of the financial assistance requested from the LGU, if not possible, since the school is not an implementing unit, official receipt of the Dep-Ed Division of Masbate Province who has control and supervision over the recipient school;
Refrain from payment of assistance directly to officials/employees of recipient schools and instead pay directly in the name of the school to monitor accountability of public funds; and
Strictly adhere to the laws, rules and regulations in the receipts and disbursement of financial assistance to public schools.

We recommended that Management expedite the filing of application for fidelity bond of all the AOs of the Municipality whose duties permit or require possession, custody or control of local government funds and properties, and consider increasing the bonds, if necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer immediately submit the subject CY 2021 DVs with complete supporting documents, collections accounts and necessary monthly reports including the lacking DVs for the current year and prior years. Otherwise, the subject disbursements will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officials and employees to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of perfected infrastructure contracts and their supporting documents to COA for a timely and complete conduct of its auditorial and technical review.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly adhere to the above cited provisions on the procurement of infrastructure projects done through public bidding;
Require the contractor to post a warranty security in place of retention money for the period supposed to be covered by a warranty security before releasing the retention money to the contractor and ensure that the defects liability period and the warranty period are both secured; and
Direct concerned officials to ensure that procurement for infrastructure projects and related payments are properly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officials to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 5 of NDMRRC, DBM, AND DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and Section 4.3 of NDRRMC, DBM, DILG and CSC JMC No. 2014-1 particularly on the allocation and utilization of LDRRMF and to submit satisfactory justification/legal basis to support disbursements of the LDRRMF amounting to ₱1,000,000.00.

We recommended and reiterated that Management:
Draw a check from the GF to transfer the unexpended amount to the trust fund to ensure funds will not be utilized for purposes other than DRRM purposes;
Require the MDRRMO to include the unexpended prior years’ DRRMF QRF and MF-MOOE in the preparation of the MDRRMF Investment Plan and pursue the implementation of unimplemented projects, otherwise, reassess the necessity of re-programming it to other projects or purposes which will be more effective in strengthening capacities of the LGU and its community; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain separate SLs for each allocation on 30 per cent QRF and 70 per cent DRRMF-MOOE by year, ensure that all required disclosure in the Notes to FS on inventories, PPEs and Special Trust Fund for LDRRMF are complied with, and that all fund transfers from various sources intended for DRRM programs, projects and activities are taken up as Trust Liability-DRRM in the Trust Fund books.

We recommended that Management require the GFPS to conduct gender analysis using available tools, such as the HGDG tool, during the planning/budgeting phase to identify existing gender issues and applicable GAD mandates, the corresponding programs/projects/activities or GAD interventions to address the same and determine actual expenditures that can be attributed to GAD budget.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure participation of the majority of the members of LSB in the formulation of SEF budget yearly and the formulation of three- year program of SEF budget taking into consideration the factors and other criteria indicated in the planning and budgeting for the SEF and incorporating the SIP and DEDP in collaboration with stakeholders in the community; and
Strictly comply with the guidelines on the planning and budgeting of SEF in accordance with Section 5 of JC No. 1 s. 2017.

We recommended that Management prioritize utilization of the remaining BGCM funds to fully implement the identified PPAs during the State of Calamity and prepare, submit to oversight agencies and post the report of utilization and status of implementation as required in LBC No. 125. If remained unutilized during the period of State of Calamity until September 12, 2022, unless lifted earlier or further extended, remaining funds should be returned to the National Treasury.
Likewise, we recommended that Management submit the lacking disbursement vouchers and lacking supporting documents as well as justification/explanation on the noted deficiencies.

We recommended and continuously reiterated that Management:
a. Compel the former OIC-MT to update his cash books and to deposit the
full amount of his undeposited collections and fully settle or refund his
unliquidated cash advances;
b. Fully effect the order dated June 30, 2018 to withhold all the money due to
the said AO, not just his salaries;
c. Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a final and proper
accounting of all his accountabilities, with bank reconciliation statements
and complete and updated subsidiary records, and adjust the Receivables-
Disallowances and Charges account to present the correct amount;
d. Compel the concerned persons liable in the Notices of Disallowances and
Notices of Finality of Decision to fully settle their liabilities; and
e. Prioritize the institution of administrative and/or legal action against the
said AO on account of his undeposited collections, unliquidated cash
advances and other offenses committed in the performance of his duties.

We reiterated and recommended the following:
a. That the Office of the Municipal Accountant give utmost attention to the
preparation and submission of the required bank reconciliation statements
pursuant to Section 474 of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160 and COA Circular
No.96-011 dated October 2, 1996 and substantiate the unreconciled
difference substantially amounting to ₱7.33 million;
b. That the Acting Municipal Treasurer prepare and maintain updated cash
books and reconcile the same with the Accounting records;
c. Any error and reconciling item discovered in the reconciliation process
must accordingly be adjusted in the respective records which need
correction/revision, to arrive at a correct and reliable cash in bank
balance; and
d. Consider closing bank account/s which is/are deemed unnecessary or a
duplication of an existing account.

We recommended that Management:
a. Institute measures to monitor its cash balances and implement additional
revenue collection strategies and austerity measures to increase collection
and improve its cash position;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to review and reconcile the composition
of payables to determine the validity of claims and accuracy of the
amounts;
c. Give priority to pay all outstanding trust liabilities to BIR, GSIS, Pag-Ibig,
and Philhealth to avoid the incurrence of penalties and surcharges; and
d. Ensure the funds received are to be used solely for the intended purpose.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Inventory Committee to continuously make utmost effort to
conduct a complete and detailed physical inventory of all the LGU’s
properties in accordance with the new guidelines and procedures
prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31 2020, prepare
and submit, within the deadlines set, the RPCPPE together with the
RPCRNS of the LGU;
b. Instruct the General Services Officer to prepare and maintain the Local
Road Inventory Map and Property Cards for each type of properties as
basis for reconciliation with the PPE accounts with the accounting records;
c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain, in relation to
the local road networks of the LGU, Local Road Network Ledger Card
(LRNLC), including the regular property ledger cards, also as basis for
reconciliation with the General Ledger account balances and with the
property records;
d. Instruct the Municipal Accountant draw a journal entry voucher and
record the transfer of the completed infrastructure projects, complete with
the required documentations to present fairly the balances of Construction
in Progress and appropriate PPE accounts and consequently recognize the
depreciation expenses pertaining thereto;
e. Require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify to the proper Inventory
account semi-expendable items with estimated useful life of more than one
year but less than the capitalization threshold and recorded as PPE. These
items must be recorded as expense upon their issuance and tender of
corresponding Inventory Custodian Slip to the end-user for proper
monitoring, control and accountability. Also, capitalizable expenditures
erroneously recorded as Repairs and Maintenance must be adjusted and
recorded into appropriate PPE accounts and to be provided with
41
depreciation expense; and
f. Require the GSO and Municipal Accountant to make a final reconciliation
of their respective records and avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account
balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and
adjust the books accordingly, in order to come up with a reliable PPE
balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that Management require:
a. The Inventory Committee to conduct a physical count to determine the
existing and serviceable insurable properties of the LGU;
b. The General Services Office (i) to submit an inventory list of insurable
properties; and (ii) to secure insurance of all its insurable property from
the GSIS in order to mitigate possible losses on the part of the
Municipality; and
c. The Budget Office to ensure that appropriate amount is included in the
budget to cover for the property insurance premiums.

We recommended the following to Management:
a. Adherence to Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 on the withholding and
remittance, intact, in full amount, and within the prescribed period of
taxes due to the national government; and
b. Require the concerned officials to trace existence of abnormal balances,
reconcile its records and immediately remit the correct payables to enable
the government to allocate resources for priority development programs.

We recommended that Management:
a. Remit fully and timely the amount withheld from its employees to GSIS,
Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth to avoid incurring any interest or penalties due
to delayed remittances;
b. Ascertain the details of unremitted prior year/s balances through
reconciliation of their records with the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth
and remit immediately the adjusted balance to the respective agency and
effect the necessary adjustment/s in the books of accounts; and
c. Require the Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for the account of
GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal
Treasurer exert efforts to reconcile the reciprocal Due to LGUs and Due from LGUs
accounts between the Municipality and the Province and Barangays.
A journal entry voucher must be prepared to take up in the books of the
Municipality any adjustments noted during the reconciliation process.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management instruct the Officials
concerned to immediately release, without any condition, the shares from RPT
collections of the remaining Barangays and the Provincial Government which are
already due for payment, and subsequently release the same on a quarterly basis as
required under Section 271 of RA No. 7160 and existing rules.

We recommended that Management:
a. Establish guidelines on the utilization of communication allowance to
specify who are those persons entitled and to limit load allowances granted
to officials and employees of the agency in accordance with laws, rules and
regulations; and
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to pay the load allowances thru payroll
and not thru reimbursement on a piecemeal basis to avoid varied claims
for communication allowances and submit the lacking documents in
compliance with the rules and regulations set forth in COA Circular 2012-
001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Properly and correctly compute the depreciation expense taking into
account the required 5% residual value of PPEs;
b. Always prepare the lapsing schedule in computing and recording the
depreciation expense;
c. Ensure that all depreciable assets are provided with depreciation; and
d. Draw a journal entry voucher for the unrecorded and/or excessive prior
years’ depreciation expense to presently fairly the financial statements in
accordance with IPSAS 17.

We recommended that Management:
a. Ensure that payments for supplies and materials as well as other operating
expenses be made directly to suppliers and, if unavoidable, limit payments
done on a reimbursement basis to petty expenses and extremely necessary
and urgent needs of the LGU; and
b. Comply with the requirements of adequate procurement planning in
accordance with Section 7 of RA No. 9184 that will regulate procurement
activities and discourage reimbursement of expenses, and abide with the
procedural guidelines for shopping or small value procurement, as
applicable.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to strictly perform the duties and
functions of his office as enumerated in the Local Government Code and
other pertinent rules and laws, particularly, in ensuring that every
disbursement is supported with complete and necessary supporting
documents;
b. Install measures and strengthen the internal control system related to the
processing, recording and reporting of financial transactions; and
c. Require the concerned persons to immediately submit the required
supporting documents of the subject transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the In Charge of the
Office (ICO)-Municipal Treasurer immediately submit the August to December 2020
collection accounts and necessary monthly reports including the lacking DVs for CY
2019 and CY 2020. Otherwise, the subject disbursements will be suspended or
disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management refrain from utilizing the Special
Education Fund for programs/projects/activities that were not approved by the Local
School Board. The amounts utilized that were not in accordance with the approved
budget must be reconstituted to the fund in order not to deprive the stakeholders of
the benefits they may derive therefrom. Utmost compliance with the required SEF
rules and regulations is enjoined.

We recommended that Management:
a. Create a special trust fund account for the unexpended prior years’
DRRM funds and draw a check from the General Fund to transfer the
unexpended amount to the trust fund to ensure funds will not be utilized
for purposes other than DRRM purposes;
b. Observe judicious management of DRRM funds, to include planning,
budgeting, and optimal utilization, that is geared towards being disaster-
prepared and resilient and be in line with the government’s effort to
curtail/eliminate/mitigate the spread of COVID-19 in the community; and
c. Pursue the implementation of unimplemented projects, otherwise, reassess
the necessity of re-programming it to other projects or purposes which will
be more effective in strengthening capacities of the LGU and its
community.

We recommended and reiterated that Management:
Fully effect the withholding order dated June 30, 2018 to withhold all the money due to the said AO;
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a final and proper accounting of all his accountabilities, with bank reconciliation statements and complete and updated subsidiary records, and make a demand to immediately deposit collections and liquidate or refund his cash advances; and,
Prioritize the institution of administrative and/or legal action against the said AO on account of his undeposited collections, unliquidated cash advances and other offenses committed in the performance of his duties.

We recommended that Management establish an effective and efficient internal control on supply and property management, specifically on the: a) centralization of custody, handling and issuance of supplies and materials in the GSO; b) preparation and regular submission of the SSMI to the Municipal Accountant for the timely recognition of expenses; and c) complete conduct of the required physical count of all supplies and properties. We further recommended securing certifications from recipients of previously booked supplies and materials that no longer exist per physical count to serve as bases for the dropping of the non-existent balances from the books of accounts.

We reiterated the following recommendations:
Establish an effective and efficient Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of the LGU’s PPE, including inventory items;
Require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to establish and maintain an updated Property and Stock Cards and Property and Supplies Ledger Cards, respectively, per category and type on all PPE and Inventory items as recorded in the books of accounts, ensure the maintenance of the same for the recent years’ procurements and continuously work on the old ones; and
Require the Offices of the GSO and Accounting to reconcile their respective records and give utmost priority in producing a reconciled RPCPPE with the book balance.

We recommended that Management require the immediate submission of the lacking DV and the complete supporting documents prescribed by COA Circular No. 2012-001 on the purchase transaction, otherwise, it will be subject to audit suspensions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert effort to identify the sources of the difference between the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts through reconciliation and effect immediately the adjustments upon verification. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant must also determine if the purposes for which the transfers between funds were made have already been achieved or completed, hence must be liquidated and recorded in the separate books of accounts to present fairly the balances of the said accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management commit to faithfully remit trust collection, exert efforts in reconciling and establishing the unremitted trust collections from prior years’ transactions and once established, accordingly remit the same to the respective government agencies.

We recommended that Management instruct the officials concerned to release the Barangays’ and Province’s shares on RPT collections on a quarterly basis as required under Section 271 of RA No. 7160 and existing rules and to immediately release the said shares for CY 2019 and prior years without any condition.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to immediately prepare and submit the liquidation reports with complete supporting documents relative to the utilization of trust funds received from these NGAs. It must be assured that proper reporting and accounting is made on the utilization of the fund transfers to fairly present financial transactions at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace the period or date of acquisition of the subject accounts and specifically compute the depreciation expense for each type of asset under each account, based on the estimated useful life of assets as provided under COA Circular No. 2003-007 and the requirement of at least 5 percent residual value provided under IPSAS. Accordingly, the Accounting Office must adjust the books to properly reflect the net book value of the PPE accounts.

We recommended that Management justify the consultancy services paid during CY 2019 and explain why it did not follow the rules and regulations on hiring of consultants. The Municipal Accountant is required to immediately submit sufficient and complete documents on such payments, otherwise, they will be subjected to audit suspensions.
Any future engagement with consultants must be thoroughly assessed to avoid unnecessary expenditures for functions which are only duplication or can be provided by the existing plantilla personnel. It must undergo the proper procurement process under RA No. 9184 and payments therefor must be supported with proper and adequate documentation.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit the Disbursement Vouchers with complete supporting documents and other lacking CY 2019 accounts for our verification and post-audit, otherwise, we will be compelled to issue the corresponding suspensions/disallowances on the subject transactions.
Strict compliance with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor, within the first ten days of the ensuing month, as required by the abovementioned existing law, rules and regulations, is once again emphasized.
Management and the concerned officials and employees must be reminded of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of its inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause. Management must initiate necessary administrative and/or criminal action in case of unjustified failure/refusal to effect compliance with the foregoing requirements by subordinate officials.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately take actions to address the weaknesses in the internal control system for processing of transactions, its recording and reporting. He must strengthen the processing/pre-audit activities, avoid control overrides, and ensure that transactions processed, paid and recorded are duly supported with complete, proper and sufficient documentation, approved and properly signed by authorized officials. The Municipal Accountant and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer must:
Immediately submit the lacking DVs and liquidation reports with their corresponding supporting documents and submit the proper and complete supporting documents of the DVs on the subject transactions, otherwise, suspensions/disallowances in audit shall be issued; and
Strictly abide by their respective duties and responsibilities as provided in the Local Government Code and other existing and applicable laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management continuously adopt the implementation of the 20 percent DF PPAs in an efficient and effective manner and determine any areas therein that may still be improved. Management must also ensure to revert the savings generated in the implementation of Development Projects and re-appropriate the same to finance only those development PPAs identified to be funded under the 20 per cent DF for the ensuing year.

We recommended that Management require the Chairperson of GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) and other concerned officials to implement all the projects and activities programmed in the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) to ensure the optimum utilization of the GAD Funds for the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD focal person to submit a copy of the Annual GDPB and the corresponding Accomplishment Report to COA within the prescribed period for the timely audit so that recommended corrective measures may also be issued in the appropriate time.

observations.managementAction: Continuous demand by the Management to the former OIC-MT to deposit the amount.
observations.reason: The former OIC-MT’s undeposited collection even increased up to ₱7.26 million.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Require the recipient entities to submit utilization reports and/or status of implementation of the programs, projects or activities to which the fund transferred are intended for to ensure transparency and accountability; and

Strictly comply with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 on the Rules and Regulations in the Grant, Utilization and Liquidation of Funds Transferred to Implementing Agencies, particularly the responsibilities of the source agency and the implementing agency.
observations.managementAction: No management action.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management revert the savings generated in the implementation of Development Projects and re-appropriate the same to finance only those development programs, projects or activities identified to be funded under the 20% Development Fund for the ensuing year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
The concerned Municipal Officials undertake appropriate action to enforce the general revision of appraisal and assessment of real property for tax purposes for better and improved revenue generation; and

Strictly implement the provision of Section 171, RA No. 7160 to ensure the accuracy of business tax collected and penalize erring business taxpayers by requiring them to pay tax deficiencies including applicable interests and surcharges; and

Intensify collection of RPT and SET from delinquent property owners by adopting measures and strategies on how to improve collection efficiency and apply measures in Sections 254 and 258 of RA No. 7160 particularly to those with delinquent accounts of more than two years.
observations.reason: Although revenue had increased in CY 2018, there are still revenues forgone particularly in business taxes due to inability to intensify collection and comply with the LGC and their Revenue Code.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following and the Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to have all undeposited collections as of December 31, 2016 deposited immediately intact and in full with the authorized government depository bank;

Require the collectors to remit their collections daily and the Liquidating Officer to deposit within the prescribed period to avoid incurrence of shortages and/or possible misuse of public funds; and

Require the Accounting Office to ensure regular reconciliation of balances and prepare subsidiary ledgers for all accountable officers to properly and correctly determine respective accountabilities; and

Institute appropriate administrative and/or legal actions against the accountable officers for non-compliance with the above-cited regulations, if warranted.
observations.reason: Undeposited collection even increased in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Office of the Municipal Accountant give preferential attention on the preparation and submission of the required bank reconciliation statements pursuant to Section 474 of RA No. 7160 and COA Circular No.96-011 dated October 2, 1996 and substantiate the unreconciled amount of ₱1,542,063.39; That the cash books of the Municipal Treasurer should be regularly updated and shall preferably be considered in the preparation of the bank reconciliation statements to ensure that any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted; Any error and reconciling item discovered in the reconciliation process shall accordingly be adjusted in the respective records which need correction/revision, to arrive at a correct and reliable cash in bank balance.
observations.managementAction: No action on the part of the Accountant.
observations.reason: The bank reconciliation was still not prepared by the Accountant, thus the unreconciled amount of ₱23.61 million as of December 31, 2018 is not substantiated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Government endeavor to further improve its process in recording and overall management of RPT Receivables through the following strategies: Require the Municipal Assessor, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to strictly follow the proper process in recording of RPT Receivables as prescribed under Section 248 of RA No. 7160 and Section 20, Volume I of New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units; Require the Municipal Accountant to credit accounts RPT Receivables upon collection pursuant to Sections 24 and 25, Volume III of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System; Cause the posting of a Notice of Delinquency in the Payment of Real Property Tax at the main entrance of the Municipal Hall and in other publicly accessible place and conspicuous place in each barangay of the municipality in accordance with Section 254 of RA No. 7160; and Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain a subsidiary ledger pertaining to RPT Receivables.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant was still not able to establish the RPT receivable at the beginning of the year. Collection is directly credited to Income account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to require the Municipal Engineer and MPDO to provide the Office of the Auditor at the beginning of the year a list of all on-going and PPAs to be implemented during the year. Further, a monthly monitoring report on the status of the projects should be submitted instead of its quarterly report. Likewise, the Municipal Engineer should see to it that verification of the projects’ status as reported in its monthly report in accordance with the frequency of verification provided in Item 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 be timely requested and the necessary documents be submitted to facilitate the inspection by COA-TAS.
observations.managementAction: No management action.
observations.reason: The list of all on-going PPAs and those to be implementing during the year were still not submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the following: The Municipal Accountant shall ensure completeness of documentation on SEF utilizations by requiring their submission from concerned DepEd officials; and The Head of Office restrain DepEd officials concerned from charging expenses that are not within the bounds of laws and regulations on SEF.
observations.managementAction: No management action.
observations.reason: The documents for the subject disbursement were still not submitted to the Audit Team. Further, current year disbursements for the same transactions still lacked the necessary and required supporting documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following to the Management:
Require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to maintain an updated Property and Stock Cards and Property and Supplies Ledger Cards, respectively, per category and type on all PPE and Inventory items as recorded in the books of accounts, ensure the maintenance of the same for the recent years’ procurements and continuously work on the old ones;
Require the Inventory Committee to complete the physical count of the Municipality’s PPE, including the inventory items; and
Require both offices to reconcile property records with the accounting records and give priority in coming up with a reconciled RPCPPE and RPCI with the corresponding book balances.

We recommended and reiterated that:
The Municipal Accountant and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer must strictly perform the duties and responsibilities of their respective offices in accordance with those enumerated in the Local Government Code. The recording of financial transactions and preparation of the books of accounts must be the sole responsibility of the Municipal Accountant and must not be assigned to the OIC-Municipal Treasurer regardless of any reason there can be;
The Management, particularly the Municipal Accountant, must install measures and strengthen the internal control system related to the processing, recording and reporting of financial transactions;
The Municipal Accountant must strictly perform the duties and functions of his office as are enumerated in the Local Government Code and other pertinent rules and laws, particularly the following:
Preparation and recording of transactions initially in the required journals before they are posted to the ledgers,
Prepare subsidiary ledgers, with priority to those sensitive accounts which determines accountability such as Cash In Local Treasury and Advances accounts, and those which require regular reconciliation such as Cash In Bank and PPE accounts,
Ensure that every disbursement is supported with complete and necessary supporting documents,
Conduct period reconciliation of accounts, giving priority to the Cash In Bank and PPE accounts due to existence of substantial unreconciled balances; and
The OIC-Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant must immediately submit the aforementioned accounts to avoid charges, suspensions or disallowances in audit.

We recommended and reiterated that the Management,
Require the former OIC-Municipal Treasurer to have all his undeposited collections as of December 31, 2018 deposited immediately intact and in full with the authorized government depository bank; and
Institute appropriate administrative and/or legal actions against the former OIC-MT for non-compliance with the above-cited regulations.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Management,
Demand immediate settlement/liquidation of the outstanding cash advances of the ICO-Municipal Treasurer, and all other accountable officers, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002;
Require the Municipal Accountant to strictly monitor the grant and liquidation of cash advances and avoid granting additional CAs unless the previous cash advance is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made pursuant to Section 89 of PD No. 1445; and
Institute appropriate administrative and/or legal action against the ICO-Municipal Treasurer for the immediate settlement of these cash advances. Otherwise, we will be compelled to exhaust the said legal remedies for violating Article 217 and 218 of the Revised Penal Code and Section 89 of P.D. 1445 which is punishable under Section 128 of the same Code.

We recommended that Management require the Office of the Municipal Accountant and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to fast track the submission of the said liquidation reports complete with their respective supporting documents to facilitate timely review and verification of the utilization of cash advances, otherwise, the corresponding suspensions/disallowances on the subject disbursements will be issued.
The Management and the concerned officials and employees is also reminded of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of its inability to and/or complacence in rendering accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to update the cashbooks daily and reconcile the cashbook balance with the accounting records every end of the month. Make the necessary corrections should there be a difference between these two records.
In all instances, we recommended strict observance of the provisions of Section 181 of the GAAM and Section 63 of the Local Treasury Operations Manual as these are important control tools for cash.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to:
Maintain a Check Register where all checks issued must be recorded chronologically and where the claimants shall be required to acknowledge receipt pursuant to Section 42 of NGAS Volume I;
Prepare daily the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) of the checks released to claimants for each fund which must be submitted to the Municipal Accountant as basis for the preparation of Journal Entry Voucher and subsequently for recording in the Check Disbursement Journal; and,
Record all checks issued including cancelled checks chronologically in the RCI.

We recommended that Management:
Exert effort to validate/establish the recorded amount of Receivable accounts;
Require all Municipal personnel with outstanding cash advances who are about to retire, resign or transfer to other agency to settle first their cash and property accountabilities before granting them the necessary clearance; and
Request for write-off of dormant account receivable, unliquidated cash advances and fund transfer accounts which remained non-moving for 10 years or more and where settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained in accordance with the guidelines and procedure provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for fair presentation of the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw a journal entry voucher and record the transfer of the above completed infrastructure projects consistent with the accounting for construction projects under the NGAS for LGUs, complete with the required documentations to present fairly the balances of Construction in Progress and appropriate asset accounts and consequently recognize the depreciation expenses pertaining thereto.

We recommended that the Municipality:
Through the Municipal Treasurer, strictly follow the procedural guidelines prescribed in the Revenue Code and the IRR of the Local Government Code for the termination or retirement of business;
Strictly implement the provision of Section 171 of the Local Government Code to ensure the accuracy of business tax collected and penalize erring business taxpayers by requiring them to pay tax deficiencies including applicable interests and surcharges; and
Collect the taxes due from these 75 business establishments, including any interest, surcharges or penalties for non-payment or delay in payment.

We recommended that the LGU thru the Municipal Treasurer’s Office conduct inspection of the business establishments that are not included in the database of the Municipality to actually validate the existence of the business establishments. Require the immediate imposition of business taxes, fees and charges and explanation from owners/proprietors on operating without the benefit of business permits. When necessary and in accordance with the provision of the Revenue Code of the Municipality and the Local Government Code and its IRR, file appropriate charges against business establishments operating without the benefit of permits as required.
We also suggested that the Municipal Treasurer strictly enforce the requirement of Section 152 of the LGC where barangay clearances are to be submitted by business establishments before business taxes are paid and permits are issued.

We recommended to the Management to require the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare and submit to the GPPB the procurement monitoring report within the time frame as stipulated in Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184, so that monitoring and evaluation of the LGUs compliance to the provisions of the Procurement Act can be made. Moreover, we also suggested to furnish our office a copy of such report for information and reference in audit.

We recommended that Management,
Refrain from utilizing the Special Education Fund for non-related programs/projects. The utilized amounts for non-related expenses must be restituted to the fund in order not to deprive the stakeholders of the benefits they may derive therefrom; and
Require Local School Board to utilize the SEF for the priority and allowable programs, projects and activities enumerated in Sections 100 (c) and 272 of the Local Government Code and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01 s. 2017 to ensure the proper utilization of the fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board to convene as soon as possible to update and finalize the 10-Year SWM Plan, prepare and submit the documentary requirements to hasten the approval of the plan by the National Solid Waste Management Commission so that not only will the planned programs, projects, or activities be in harmony with the National Solid Waste Management Framework but also to ensure that total protection of the environment and the general welfare of its constituents in harmony with economic development will be attained. Further, the Management must also set aside a reasonable appropriation in their annual budgets under the Environmental Development portion of the 20% Development Fund projects to provide funds for the implementation of the PPAs included in its SWMP.

We recommended that the LDRRM Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer, strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines for the LDRRMF set forth in COA Circular No. 2012-002, such as the preparation of the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, the Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) and subsidiary ledgers for DRRMF in the general fund and in the trust fund, classified by source and by year.

We recommended the following:
The Municipal Accountant must ensure that the unexpended balance of QRF and MF-MOOE for each year be timely transferred at year-end; and,
The Accounting Office must coordinate with the Budget Office to determine if cash back-up is still available for the above unexpended balances, rule out the unexpended balance beyond the last five years, reconcile their records and obtain the correct amount of the unexpended balances and effect the transfer accordingly.

We recommended that Management:
In the succeeding year’s preparation of Annual Gender and Development Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report, the schedule in GAD Planning and Budgeting expressly stated in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 must be observed to conform with the law and regulations governing GAD;
Submit a copy of the Annual Gender and Development Plan and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report to COA within the prescribed period for the timely audit so that recommended corrective measures may also be issued in the appropriate time; and
Establish and maintain GAD database to serve as basis in the identification of priority gender issues that are crucial for the Municipality’s evidence-based planning and policy making.

observations.recommendation:

Prepare and submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report of LGU vehicles and heavy equipment to the Commission on Audit (thru Municipal Accountant) for verification and evaluation of the Auditor within 10 days of the ensuing month; and

Establish and implement effective control over the use of government vehicle and fuel, oil and lubricants consumption to prevent wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures; and
